it.title: EUDR (EU Deforestation Regulation)
it.full_brief:
# EUDR (EU Deforestation Regulation) — Regulatory Fact Document

## Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document provides a workspace-anchored regulatory intelligence brief on Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 31 May 2023 on the making available on the Union market and the export from the Union of certain commodities and products associated with deforestation and forest degradation (EUDR), as amended by Regulation (EU) 2024/3234 and Regulation (EU) 2025/2650.

**Coverage:** This brief addresses the regulation's full substantive requirements as enacted, the December 2024 and December 2025 amendment effects on application dates, the country benchmarking system, the information system, due diligence obligations, registration and reporting, penalties, exemptions, and the current forward regulatory calendar. All requirements are drawn from the primary enacted text and corroborating official sources. Every qualification, carve-out, and defined term material to the workspace is captured.

**Convention notes:**
- FACT claims are span-grounded to a source block URL listed in this document or the New Sources Identified table.
- ANALYSIS claims are prefixed with one of four closed-set labels: *Analytical inference:*, *Operational implication:*, *Industry interpretation:*, or *Per the workspace's reading:*.
- Legal conclusions are routed to *Legal Confirmation Required:* callouts.
- The workspace is referenced as "the workspace" or by operational role. No company or individual name is used.
- Cut-off date referenced throughout: 31 December 2020.
- Application dates reflect the December 2025 amendment (Regulation (EU) 2025/2650).

---

## What This Regulation Is and Why It Applies to the Workspace

### Plain-Language Summary

FACT: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 "lays down rules regarding the placing and making available on the Union market as well as the export from the Union of relevant products, as listed in Annex I, that contain, have been fed with or have been made using relevant commodities, namely cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soya and wood." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 1(1), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: The regulation was adopted on 31 May 2023 and "entered into force on the twentieth day following that of its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union," which is 29 June 2023. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 38(1), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: Application of Articles 3–13, 16–24, 26, 31, and 32 was originally set at 30 December 2024. As amended by Regulation (EU) 2024/3234 (postponing by one year) and Regulation (EU) 2025/2650 (postponing by a further year), "Operators, downstream operators and traders that are not micro- or small enterprises will have to comply with the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 from 30 December 2026, and micro- and small enterprises from 30 June 2027." *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html

FACT: The regulation repeals Regulation (EU) No 995/2010 (the EU Timber Regulation) with effect from 30 December 2024, subject to transitional provisions for timber produced before 29 June 2023. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 37(1), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Jurisdictional Scope

FACT: The regulation applies to: (a) products placed on or made available on the Union market; and (b) products exported from the Union. FACT: By operation of Article 7, "Where a natural or legal person established outside the Union places relevant products on the market, the first natural or legal person established in the Union who makes such relevant products available on the market shall be deemed to be an operator within the meaning of this Regulation." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 7, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating as freight forwarders, customs brokers, or logistics intermediaries handling shipments of covered commodities or derived products into or out of the EU, the regulation's scope extends to the first EU-established person making the product available — meaning that role assignment in the supply chain has direct compliance consequences that cannot be resolved without legal review.

### Why This Regulation Is Relevant to the Workspace

The workspace, operating in freight forwarding and logistics across air, road, and ocean modes with trade lanes spanning the Americas, Europe, and Asia, handles cargo that is likely to include Annex I-listed commodities and derived products — wood-based packaging, furniture, rubber components, cocoa or coffee products, and palm oil derivatives are all within the EUDR's product list. The workspace's role as customs agent, importer's representative, or forwarder means it may be embedded in supply chains where the regulation imposes obligations on its clients and, depending on the structure of individual transactions, potentially on the workspace itself.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in any specific transaction, meets the definition of "operator" ("any natural or legal person who, in the course of a commercial activity, places relevant products on the market or exports them") or "trader" ("any person in the supply chain other than the operator who, in the course of a commercial activity, makes relevant products available on the market") requires transaction-by-transaction legal review. These definitions govern which obligations attach. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(15) and Article 2(17), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* For air-primary workspaces, express and air freight shipments of wood-based packaging materials (HS 4415), rubber goods (HS 4011–4016), coffee (HS 0901), cocoa and chocolate (HS 1801–1806), soy derivatives, palm oil derivatives, and wood furniture (HS 9403) are within the Annex I product list. Each of these is a common air cargo category. The workspace's clients placing these products on the EU market will require documentary support from the workspace to fulfil the due diligence statement reference number requirement at the customs interface.

---

## Issues Requiring Immediate Action

### WINDOW CLOSING — Information System Registration

FACT: "The Information System as set out in Art. 33 of the Regulation was launched on 4 December 2024. Registration for users of the system was opened on November 2024." *Source: European Commission DG Environment — Regulation on Deforestation-free Products, European Commission, accessed 2026. https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/forests/deforestation/regulation-deforestation-free-products_en

*Operational implication:* Operators and traders placing covered products on the EU market from 30 December 2026 are expected to submit due diligence statements through this information system. Workspaces whose clients qualify as operators or non-SME traders should advise those clients to register now; workspaces that may themselves act as operators or authorised representatives should also assess whether registration is required for their own role.

**Action:** Identify clients by cargo vertical whose shipments contain Annex I commodities or products. Advise immediate registration in the EUDR Information System for those clients who are operators. Confirm with legal counsel whether the workspace itself must register as an operator or authorised representative in any transaction structure.

**Deadline:** 30 December 2026 for large and medium operators and traders; 30 June 2027 for micro and small enterprises. Registration is open now.

### ACTION REQUIRED — Due Diligence Statement Readiness

FACT: "Operators shall not place relevant products on the market or export them without prior submission of a due diligence statement." FACT: The due diligence statement must contain: operator name, address, and EORI number (for products entering/leaving the market); HS code; description and quantity of the relevant product; country of production; geolocation of all plots of land where relevant commodities were produced; and a declaration that due diligence was exercised and no or only a negligible risk was found. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 4(2) and Annex II, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* For workspaces handling customs declarations, the regulation requires that "the person lodging the customs declaration for release for free circulation or export of a relevant product shall make available to customs authorities the reference number of the due diligence statement assigned to that relevant product by the information system." This creates a pre-clearance data requirement that the workspace must be able to support operationally.

**Action:** Review customs documentation workflows. Add a pre-clearance check for EUDR Annex I commodity codes. Build a client-facing process to collect due diligence statement reference numbers before lodging customs declarations.

### COST ALERT — Benchmarking-Linked Compliance Asymmetry

FACT: The regulation establishes a three-tier risk classification system for countries (low, standard, high risk). For high-risk countries: competent authorities check at least 9% of operators and 9% of the quantity of relevant products. For standard-risk countries: at least 3% of operators. For low-risk countries: at least 1% of operators. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Articles 16(8)–(10), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* Supply chains sourcing from high-risk countries face a ninefold higher inspection rate than low-risk-country supply chains. For workspaces handling cargo from major producing regions in Southeast Asia (palm oil, rubber), West Africa (cocoa, coffee), South America (soy, cattle), the benchmarking classification of those countries directly determines how frequently shipments will be subject to detailed competent-authority examination and potential customs suspension.

**Action:** Map each client's country of production against the benchmarking implementing act (Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1093) to identify which shipment corridors face enhanced scrutiny. This mapping determines which client relationships need the most intensive due diligence support investment.

### MONITORING — Delegated Act on Product Scope

FACT: The World Resources Institute reports that the European Commission has published "a draft Delegated Act to adjust the product scope; notably, the Commission proposes removing leather and retreaded tires from the regulation while potentially adding instant coffee and certain palm oil derivatives. This draft is now open for public consultation until June 1, 2026." *Source: World Resources Institute — What Is the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)?, WRI, last updated 15 May 2026. https://www.wri.org/insights/explain-eu-deforestation-regulation

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces handling leather goods (HS 4107, currently in Annex I under cattle) or retreaded tires (HS 4012, currently in Annex I under rubber), the proposed removal of these product categories would reduce compliance scope. Conversely, the potential addition of instant coffee and palm oil derivatives expands scope. The consultation closed 1 June 2026; the delegated act's final form is pending.

**Re-check window:** Monitor for Commission publication of the final Delegated Act on product scope amendments; expected post-consultation Q3–Q4 2026.

---

## How the Workspace Sits in the Compliance Chain

### Defined Roles Under the Regulation

FACT: The regulation defines:
- **'operator'**: "any natural or legal person who, in the course of a commercial activity, places relevant products on the market or exports them."
- **'trader'**: "any person in the supply chain other than the operator who, in the course of a commercial activity, makes relevant products available on the market."
- **'placing on the market'**: "the first making available of a relevant commodity or relevant product on the Union market."
- **'making available on the market'**: "any supply of a relevant product for distribution, consumption or use on the Union market in the course of a commercial activity, whether in return for payment or free of charge."

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(15)–(18), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: "Where a natural or legal person established outside the Union places relevant products on the market, the first natural or legal person established in the Union who makes such relevant products available on the market shall be deemed to be an operator within the meaning of this Regulation." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 7, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: The regulation also establishes the role of **'authorised representative'**: "any natural or legal person established in the Union who, in accordance with Article 6, has received a written mandate from an operator or from a trader to act on its behalf in relation to specified tasks with regard to the operator's or the trader's obligations under this Regulation." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(22), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Role Placement — Legal Confirmation Required

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, in any given transaction, is an "operator" (first EU person making the product available, or placing it on the market or exporting it), a "trader" (any other supply-chain person making products available), or an "authorised representative" (acting under written mandate) is a legal determination that turns on the specific contractual and commercial structure of each transaction. The workspace's role as freight forwarder does not by itself determine which category applies. Each category carries materially different obligations. Counsel review is required before any compliance programme is designed or represented to clients.

### Obligation Differentiation by Role (as defined in the regulation)

FACT: Non-SME traders "shall be considered as non-SME operators and shall be subject to obligations and provisions in Articles 3, 4 and 6, Articles 8 to 13, Article 16(8) to (11) and Article 18 with regard to the relevant commodities and relevant products that they make available on the market." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 5(1), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: SME traders "shall collect and keep" specified information for at least five years from the date of making available on the market, including supplier and customer names, addresses, email addresses, and "the reference numbers of the due diligence statements associated to those products." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 5(3)–(4), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: "Operators and traders should be bound by the obligations under this Regulation regardless of whether the making available on the market takes place through traditional or online means." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Recital (30), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* For the workspace acting as customs declarant, the minimum operational requirement at the border is to have the due diligence statement reference number available for transmission to customs authorities before release for free circulation or export. This is not itself an operator obligation — it is a customs procedural requirement — but it creates a data dependency on the client's compliance status that the workspace must plan for.

---

## Authoritative Guidance Document Analysis

### Status of Official Guidance

FACT: The European Commission's Green Forum implementation hub states: "Read the official Guidance [which] explains the main terms and requirements of the EUDR, supporting anyone who needs to comply with the Regulation. It includes explanations of key definitions and roles, timelines for implementation and clarification of which products and activities are covered. It also provides guidance on performing due diligence (including with regards to legality), acknowledges the role of certification and third-party verification schemes and clarifies the term 'agricultural use'. The Guidance was developed with EU Member States' representatives and can also support national competent authorities and enforcement bodies." *Source: European Commission Green Forum — Deforestation Regulation Implementation, European Commission DG Environment, accessed 2026. https://green-forum.ec.europa.eu/nature-and-biodiversity/deforestation-regulation-implementation_en

FACT: The EUR-Lex legislative summary references "Commission Notice – Guidance document for Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 on deforestation-free products" as a related document. *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html

FACT: A regularly-updated FAQ document is available at the Commission's Green Forum hub covering "geolocation and traceability, product scope, obligations, definitions, due diligence, the EUDR Information System, timelines and penalties." *Source: European Commission Green Forum — Deforestation Regulation Implementation, European Commission DG Environment, accessed 2026. https://green-forum.ec.europa.eu/nature-and-biodiversity/deforestation-regulation-implementation_en

*Note: The full text of the official Guidance document and the FAQ were not available as complete source blocks for this brief. The analysis below is therefore drawn from the primary enacted regulation text and Commission-published summaries. For detailed guidance on specific product classifications, role determinations, and geolocation technical requirements, the workspace should retrieve and review the Commission Notice and the FAQ directly from the Green Forum hub. These are classified as Tier 2 sources (regulator guidance and interpretation) and should be treated as authoritative interpretation of the regulation.*

### Key Guidance Points Confirmed in Source Blocks

FACT: The Commission's implementing hub confirms the regulation applies from 30 December 2026 for "large and medium operators" and from 30 June 2027 for "micro and small operators." It also confirms: "Micro and small operators already covered by the EU Timber Regulation (EUTR): 30 December 2026." *Source: European Commission DG Environment — Regulation on Deforestation-free Products, European Commission, accessed 2026. https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/forests/deforestation/regulation-deforestation-free-products_en

FACT: The EUR-Lex summary confirms the amended regulation "distinguishes between operators, downstream operators and traders, with differentiated obligations along the supply chain." It further confirms: "Micro or small primary operators may fulfil this requirement through a simplified declaration. The regulation allows operators to mandate that an authorised representative submit a due diligence statement or a simplified declaration on their behalf." *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html

FACT: The Commission has published "Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1093 on a list of low- and high-risk countries under Regulation (EU) 2023/1115." This is the benchmarking implementing act. *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html

---

## Anticipated Authoritative Guidance and Pending Regulatory Events

### MONITORING — Delegated Act on Product Scope

FACT: The European Commission has published a draft Delegated Act proposing to remove leather and retreaded tires from the regulation's Annex I scope while potentially adding instant coffee and certain palm oil derivatives. The draft was open for public consultation until June 1, 2026. *Source: World Resources Institute — What Is the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)?, WRI, last updated 15 May 2026. https://www.wri.org/insights/explain-eu-deforestation-regulation

*Analytical inference:* Based on standard delegated act procedures under Article 35 of the regulation, the Commission is required to notify the European Parliament and Council simultaneously upon adoption. The Parliament and Council have two months (extendable by two months) to object. A final Delegated Act could enter into force Q3–Q4 2026, potentially before the 30 December 2026 application date.

**Re-check window:** Q3 2026. Operational implication: workspaces handling cattle leather (HS 4107) or retreaded tires (HS 4012) should monitor this closely as it could remove two Annex I categories from the compliance perimeter.

### MONITORING — Information System Improvements

FACT: The WRI reports that "The Commission is also updating the user friendliness of the EUDR Information System that companies use to submit their due diligence statements and incorporating the December amendments. Specifically, these updates introduce a simplified form for small businesses, updated automated interfaces (APIs) and a contingency plan to ensure reliability as the December deadline approaches." *Source: World Resources Institute — What Is the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)?, WRI, last updated 15 May 2026. https://www.wri.org/insights/explain-eu-deforestation-regulation

**Re-check window:** Ongoing; monitor before 30 December 2026 application date for system stability and API readiness affecting customs-IT integration.

### MONITORING — Commission Report on Administrative Burden

FACT: The European Parliament's December 2025 adoption press release states: "By 30 April 2026, the Commission must present a report to assess the law's impact and administrative burden, in particular for micro and small operators." *Source: European Parliament — Deforestation Law: Parliament Adopts Changes to Postpone and Simplify Measures, European Parliament, 17 December 2025. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20251211IPR32168/deforestation-law-parliament-adopts-changes-to-postpone-and-simplify-measures

*Analytical inference:* This report may trigger further legislative proposals on compliance simplification. The WRI confirms the Commission has already published a formal report analyzing compliance cost reduction alongside updated FAQs and guidance, and that the Commission's stated goal is to help companies implement simplified rules before the December 30 deadline rather than introduce further legislative changes to the core text.

**Re-check window:** Q2–Q3 2026 for any further simplification measures flowing from this report.

### MONITORING — Electronic Interface Between Customs and Information System

FACT: Article 28(1) of the regulation provides: "This electronic interface shall be in place by 30 June 2028." Until this interface is operational, Article 26(5)(a) applies: "paragraphs 6 to 9 of this Article shall not apply, and customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 27." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 28(1) and Article 26(5), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* The automated customs-to-information-system interface will not be in place at the December 2026 application date. The manual/cooperative exchange mechanism under Article 27 governs the interim period from December 2026 to June 2028. Workspaces should design their customs declaration workflows for the manual interim process, not the automated interface.

**Re-check window:** June 2028 target date for full electronic interface.

---

## Threshold Questions

### Question 1: Does the workspace handle Annex I products?

**Status: Factual determination required, not a legal question.** The workspace must audit its cargo manifests against Annex I HS codes. FACT: The Annex I list includes, among others: cattle products (HS 0102, 0201, 0202, 4101, 4104, 4107); cocoa and chocolate (HS 1801–1806); coffee (HS 0901); palm oil and derivatives (HS 1511, 1513, 2306, 2915, 3823); rubber products (HS 4001, 4005–4017); soya (HS 1201, 1507, 2304); and wood and wood products (HS 4401–4421, Chapters 47–48 pulp and paper, HS 9401 wooden seats, HS 9403 wooden furniture, HS 9406 prefabricated wooden buildings). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Annex I, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: Printed books, newspapers, and other printed products (Chapter 49) were removed from the scope by the December 2025 amendment. *Source: European Parliament — Deforestation Law: Parliament Adopts Changes to Postpone and Simplify Measures, European Parliament, 17 December 2025. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20251211IPR32168/deforestation-law-parliament-adopts-changes-to-postpone-and-simplify-measures

FACT: The regulation excludes used commodities and products "that have completed their lifecycle, and would otherwise be disposed of as waste, as defined in Article 3, point (1), of Directive 2008/98/EC." However, "this should not apply to certain by-products of the manufacturing process." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Recital (40) and Annex I preamble, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Question 2: Is the workspace an operator, trader, or authorised representative?

**Status: *Legal Confirmation Required*** in each transaction. The definitions of "operator" and "trader" are set out in Article 2(15) and 2(17) as quoted above. The key dividing line is whether the workspace "places" a product on the market (operator) or merely "makes available" an already-placed product further down the chain (trader). Whether a freight forwarder acting as customs declarant, importer of record, or logistics agent constitutes either role requires transaction-level legal analysis.

### Question 3: Does the SME threshold apply to the workspace?

**Status: Factual determination + *Legal Confirmation Required*.**

FACT: The regulation distinguishes obligations based on whether entities are "micro, small and medium-sized enterprises or 'SMEs' means micro, small and medium-sized undertakings as defined in Article 3 of Directive 2013/34/EU." FACT: The European Parliament's press release on the December 2025 amendment specifies: "small operators – private individuals and enterprises with under 50 employees and an annual turnover related to the products concerned of less than €10 million." *Source: European Parliament — Deforestation Law: Parliament Adopts Changes to Postpone and Simplify Measures, European Parliament, 17 December 2025. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20251211IPR32168/deforestation-law-parliament-adopts-changes-to-postpone-and-simplify-measures

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace meets the SME definition as amended (and whether the relevant turnover figure is enterprise-wide or limited to turnover "related to the products concerned") determines which tier of obligations and which application date applies. Counsel review required before the workspace categorises itself or advises clients.

### Question 4: Does the cut-off date of 31 December 2020 apply to all products the workspace handles?

**Status: Decided — verbatim from regulation text.** FACT: "'deforestation-free' means: (a) that the relevant products contain, have been fed with or have been made using, relevant commodities that were produced on land that has not been subject to deforestation after 31 December, 2020; and (b) in the case of relevant products that contain or have been made using wood, that the wood has been harvested from the forest without inducing forest degradation after 31 December, 2020." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(13), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: "This Regulation does not apply to relevant products listed in Annex I produced before the date indicated in Article 38(1)," which is 29 June 2023 (date of entry into force). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 1(2), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

---

## Substantive Requirements

### 3(a): The Three Conditions for Market Access

FACT: "Relevant commodities and relevant products shall not be placed or made available on the market or exported, unless all the following conditions are fulfilled: (a) they are deforestation-free; (b) they have been produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production; and (c) they are covered by a due diligence statement." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 3, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(b): Legality Requirement — Full Definition

FACT: "'relevant legislation of the country of production' means the laws applicable in the country of production concerning the legal status of the area of production in terms of: (a) land use rights; (b) environmental protection; (c) forest-related rules, including forest management and biodiversity conservation, where directly related to wood harvesting; (d) third parties' rights; (e) labour rights; (f) human rights protected under international law; (g) the principle of free, prior and informed consent (FPIC), including as set out in the UN Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples; (h) tax, anti-corruption, trade and customs regulations." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(40), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* This is broader than purely environmental legality. For workspaces sourcing from or handling shipments from countries with governance challenges, the legality assessment must cover labour rights, FPIC, and anti-corruption — not only land-use permits.

### 3(c): Due Diligence System Requirements

FACT: "Prior to placing relevant products on the market or exporting them, operators shall exercise due diligence with regard to all relevant products supplied by each particular supplier." FACT: Due diligence "shall include: (a) the collection of information, data and documents needed to fulfil the requirements set out in Article 9; (b) risk assessment measures as referred to in Article 10; (c) risk mitigation measures as referred to in Article 11." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 8(1)–(2), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(d): Information Requirements (Article 9) — Full Specification

FACT: Operators must collect, organise, and keep for **five years** from the date of placing on the market or export, for each relevant product:

> "(a) a description, including the trade name and type of the relevant products as well as, in the case of relevant products that contain or have been made using wood, the common name of the species and their full scientific name; the product description shall include the list of relevant commodities or relevant products contained therein or used to make those products;
> (b) the quantity of the relevant products; for relevant products entering or leaving the market, the quantity is to be expressed in kilograms of net mass and, where applicable, in the supplementary unit set out in Annex I to Council Regulation (EEC) No 2658/87 against the indicated Harmonised System code …
> (c) the country of production and, where relevant, parts thereof;
> (d) the geolocation of all plots of land where the relevant commodities … were produced, as well as the date or time range of production … for relevant products that contain or have been made using cattle … the geolocation shall refer to all the establishments where the cattle were kept; for all other relevant products of Annex I, the geolocation shall refer to the plots of land;
> (e) the name, postal address and email address of any business or person from whom they have been supplied …;
> (f) the name, postal address and email address of any business, operator or trader to whom the relevant products have been supplied;
> (g) adequately conclusive and verifiable information that the relevant products are deforestation-free;
> (h) adequately conclusive and verifiable information that the relevant commodities have been produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production, including any arrangement conferring the right to use the respective area for the purposes of the production of the relevant commodity."

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 9(1)(a)–(h), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

**Geolocation specification:** FACT: "'geolocation' means the geographical location of a plot of land described by means of latitude and longitude coordinates corresponding to at least one latitude and one longitude point and using at least six decimal digits; for plots of land of more than four hectares used for the production of the relevant commodities other than cattle, this shall be provided using polygons with sufficient latitude and longitude points to describe the perimeter of each plot of land." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(28), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(e): Risk Assessment Requirements (Article 10)

FACT: The risk assessment must take into account fourteen criteria including: the benchmarking risk classification of the country of production; presence of forests and indigenous peoples in the country; prevalence of deforestation or forest degradation; source and reliability of information; complexity of the supply chain and stage of processing; risk of circumvention or mixing with products of unknown origin; substantiated concerns; and "complementary information on compliance … which may include information supplied by certification or other third-party verified schemes, including voluntary schemes recognised by the Commission under Article 30(5) of Directive(EU) 2018/2001." FACT: "The operators shall document and review the risk assessments **at least on an annual basis** and make them available to the competent authorities upon request." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 10(2) and Article 10(4), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

**FLEGT exception:** FACT: "Wood products which fall within the scope of Regulation (EC) No 2173/2005 that are covered by a valid FLEGT license from an operational licensing scheme shall be deemed to comply with Article 3, point (b), of this Regulation." This deems FLEGT-licensed wood to satisfy the legality requirement only, not the deforestation-free condition. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 10(3), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(f): Risk Mitigation Requirements (Article 11)

FACT: Where a risk assessment reveals a non-negligible risk, operators shall adopt risk mitigation procedures "that are adequate to achieve no or only a negligible risk." FACT: For non-SME operators, risk mitigation policies must include: "(a) model risk management practices, reporting, record-keeping, internal control and compliance management, including the appointment of a **compliance officer at management level** for non-SME operators; (b) an independent audit function to check the internal policies, controls and procedures referred to in point (a) for all non-SME operators." FACT: Risk mitigation decisions "shall be documented, reviewed at least on an annual basis and made available by the operators to the competent authorities upon request." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 11(2) and Article 11(3), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(g): Due Diligence System Maintenance and Reporting (Article 12)

FACT: Operators must "review the due diligence system at least once a year." FACT: "Operators who do not fall within the categories of SMEs, including microenterprises, or natural persons shall, on an annual basis, publicly report as widely as possible, including via the internet, on their due diligence system, including on the steps taken by them to fulfil their obligations as set out in Article 8." FACT: Operators may fulfil this reporting by including it in reports under other Union due diligence legal acts (e.g., CSDDD). FACT: All documentation related to due diligence must be kept for "at least five years." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 12(2)–(5), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(h): Feed Livestock — Special Geolocation Rule

FACT: "For relevant products that contain or have been made using cattle, and for such relevant products that have been fed with relevant products, the geolocation shall refer to all the establishments where the cattle were kept; for all other relevant products of Annex I, the geolocation shall refer to the plots of land." FACT: For cattle feed, if the feed has been subject to prior due diligence, operators may use "relevant invoices, reference numbers of relevant due diligence statements or any other relevant documentation indicating that the feed is deforestation-free." "The evidence should cover the lifetime of the animals, up to a maximum of five years." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 9(1)(d) and Recital (39), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(i): Substantiated Concerns

FACT: "Operators that obtain or are made aware of relevant new information, including substantiated concerns, indicating that a relevant product that they have placed on the market is at risk of not complying with this Regulation shall immediately inform the competent authorities of the Member States in which they placed the relevant product on the market, as well as traders to whom they supplied the relevant product." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 4(5), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: "'substantiated concern' means a duly reasoned claim based on objective and verifiable information regarding non-compliance with this Regulation and which could require the intervention of competent authorities." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 2(31), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### 3(j): Customs Declaration Interface

FACT: "The reference number of the due diligence statement shall be made available to customs authorities before the release for free circulation or export of a relevant product entering or leaving the market. For that purpose, except where the due diligence statement is made available through the electronic interface … the person lodging the customs declaration for release for free circulation or export of a relevant product shall make available to customs authorities the reference number of the due diligence statement assigned to that relevant product by the information system." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 26(4), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: "The release for free circulation or export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 26(10), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

## Product-Specific Compliance Status

### Annex I Product Map — Key Categories for Freight and Logistics Contexts

The following is a factual extract from Annex I of the enacted regulation, identifying categories most likely to appear in freight forwarding cargo streams. This is not an exhaustive list.

| Commodity | Representative Product Categories | HS Codes (selected) |
|---|---|---|
| Wood | Packing cases, boxes, crates, pallets, pallet collars | 4415 |
| Wood | Sawn wood, plywood, panels, particle board, fibreboard | 4407, 4411, 4412 |
| Wood | Wooden furniture | 9403 30/40/50/60/91 |
| Wood | Builders' joinery and carpentry | 4418 |
| Rubber | New pneumatic tyres | 4011 |
| Rubber | Retreaded/used tyres (currently in scope, proposed for removal) | 4012 |
| Cattle | Leather further prepared after tanning (currently in scope, proposed for removal) | 4107 |
| Cocoa | Chocolate and food preparations | 1806 |
| Coffee | Coffee, roasted or unroasted | 0901 |
| Soya | Soya-bean oil; oilcake | 1507, 2304 |
| Oil palm | Palm oil and fractions | 1511 |

FACT: The Annex I note states: "Except for by-products of a manufacturing process, where that process involved material that was not waste as defined in Article 3, point (1), of Directive 2008/98/EC, this Regulation does not apply to goods if they are produced entirely from material that has completed its lifecycle and would otherwise have been discarded as waste as defined in Article 3, point (1), of that Directive." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Annex I preamble, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

**Packing materials exception — significant for logistics operations:** FACT: The Annex I entry for HS 4415 (packing cases, boxes, crates, drums, pallets, pallet collars) includes the parenthetical: "not including packing material used exclusively as packing material to support, protect or carry another product placed on the market." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Annex I, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* Wooden pallets and packaging used exclusively to transport other goods are excluded from the HS 4415 scope in Annex I. This is directly relevant for workspaces whose operations involve wood-based transport packaging. However, the exclusion applies only where the packing material is used "exclusively as packing material to support, protect or carry another product placed on the market" — pallets or crates that are themselves the product being sold are within scope.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* The scope of the packing materials exclusion — specifically whether third-party-owned pallets in a reusable pool, or one-way pallets purchased by the shipper, fall within "used exclusively as packing material" — requires legal review against the Commission's guidance and FAQs.

**Printed products:** FACT: Printed books, newspapers, and other printed products (HS Chapter 49) were removed from the regulation's scope by the December 2025 amendment. *Source: European Parliament — Deforestation Law: Parliament Adopts Changes to Postpone and Simplify Measures, European Parliament, 17 December 2025. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20251211IPR32168/deforestation-law-parliament-adopts-changes-to-postpone-and-simplify-measures

## Registration and Reporting Obligations

### Information System Registration

FACT: "By 30 December 2024, the Commission shall establish and subsequently shall maintain an information system which shall contain the due diligence statements made available pursuant to Article 4(2)." FACT: The information system provides for: "registration of operators and traders and their authorised representatives in the Union; for operators placing relevant products under the customs procedure 'release for free circulation' or 'export', the Economic Operators Registration and Identification (EORI) number established pursuant to Article 9 of Regulation (EU) No 952/2013, shall be included in their registration profile." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 33(1) and Article 33(2)(a), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: The information system "was launched on 4 December 2024. Registration for users of the system was opened on November 2024." *Source: European Commission DG Environment — Regulation on Deforestation-free Products, European Commission, accessed 2026. https://environment.ec.europa.eu/topics/forests/deforestation/regulation-deforestation-free-products_en

FACT: Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/3084 sets out the rules for the functioning of the information system. *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html

FACT: The information system is described as "a software application based on the Traces platform, established under Regulation (EU) 2017/625 on enforcing EU rules for the agri-food chain." *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html

### Annual Public Reporting (Non-SME Operators)

FACT: Operators who are not SMEs (including not microenterprises or natural persons) must "on an annual basis, publicly report as widely as possible, including via the internet, on their due diligence system, including on the steps taken by them to fulfil their obligations as set out in Article 8." This reporting must include: a summary of product information; the conclusions of risk assessments and mitigation measures; and, where applicable, a description of consultation with indigenous peoples and local communities. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 12(3)–(4), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### SME Trader Record-Keeping

FACT: SME traders must collect and keep for at least five years: names, addresses, emails, and web addresses of suppliers and customers; and reference numbers of associated due diligence statements. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 5(3)–(4), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Member State Reporting

FACT: "By 30 April of each year, Member States shall make available to the public and to the Commission information on the application of this Regulation during the previous calendar year," including check results, quantities of products checked, non-compliance findings, and penalties imposed. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 22(1), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

## Operational System Requirements

### System 1: Cargo HS Code Screening Against Annex I

**Scope:** All import, export, and customs brokerage workflows involving the EU market.
**Deadline:** Operational before 30 December 2026 (first application date).
**Gap from current baseline:** Standard freight management systems classify cargo by HS code but do not cross-reference Annex I. A screening layer (manual or automated) is required to flag Annex I-covered shipments at the booking or customs declaration stage.

*Operational implication:* At minimum, the workspace needs a maintained list of Annex I HS codes with amendment monitoring (the Delegated Act on product scope may add or remove codes) mapped against the cargo booking and customs declaration system. This is a data infrastructure requirement, not solely a compliance policy requirement.

### System 2: Due Diligence Statement Reference Number Collection

**Scope:** All customs declarations for Annex I products entering (release for free circulation) or leaving (export) the EU market.
**Deadline:** Required at the point of customs declaration lodgement from 30 December 2026.
**Gap:** FACT: "the person lodging the customs declaration for release for free circulation or export of a relevant product shall make available to customs authorities the reference number of the due diligence statement assigned to that relevant product by the information system." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 26(4), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* This creates a pre-customs-clearance data dependency. The workspace must establish a client-facing workflow to collect the due diligence statement reference number from the operator (client) before lodging the customs declaration. If the reference number is absent and the shipment contains Annex I products, the customs declaration cannot proceed compliantly. A standard pre-clearance instruction document and a client onboarding checklist for EUDR-covered shipments should be built now.

### System 3: Document Retention

**Scope:** For any operator-role transactions, records must be kept for five years. FACT: "Operators shall keep a record of the due diligence statements for five years from the date the statement is submitted through the information system referred to in Article 33." FACT: Operators must also keep for five years "all documentation related to due diligence, such as all records, measures and procedures pursuant to Article 8." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 4(3) and Article 12(5), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

**Gap:** Current freight document retention practices are typically structured around customs audit periods (3–4 years in most EU jurisdictions). A five-year EUDR-specific retention policy covering due diligence statements, geolocation data, supplier information, and risk assessment records must be formally adopted.

### System 4: Compliance Officer and Audit Function (Non-SME Operators)

FACT: Non-SME operators are required to have: "(a) model risk management practices, reporting, record-keeping, internal control and compliance management, including the appointment of a compliance officer at management level for non-SME operators; (b) an independent audit function to check the internal policies, controls and procedures." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 11(2), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace, if determined to be a non-SME operator in any transaction, must itself appoint a management-level compliance officer and establish an independent audit function for EUDR purposes, or whether this requirement attaches to its clients who are the operators of record, requires legal determination by role assignment per transaction.

### System 5: Substantiated Concern Response Protocol

FACT: Operators must "receive substantiated concerns from interested parties, including by electronic means, and should thoroughly investigate all substantiated concerns received." FACT: SME traders who become aware of non-compliance risks must "immediately inform the competent authorities … as well as traders to whom they supplied the relevant product." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Recital (55) and Article 5(5), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* The workspace should build an internal notification protocol for the event that it receives information indicating non-compliance in a shipment it has handled. Absent a defined process, informal receipt of such information and failure to act creates operational and reputational exposure.

### System 6: Annual Due Diligence System Review

FACT: Operators must "review the due diligence system at least once a year" and, when aware of new developments, "update the due diligence system to take account of those developments." Changes must be kept on record for five years. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 12(2), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

## Exemptions and Edge Cases

### Exemption 1: Products Produced Before Entry Into Force

FACT: "This Regulation does not apply to relevant products listed in Annex I produced before the date indicated in Article 38(1)," which is 29 June 2023. *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 1(2), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Exemption 2: Recycled/Waste-Derived Products

FACT: The regulation excludes goods "produced entirely from material that has completed its lifecycle and would otherwise have been discarded as waste as defined in Article 3, point (1), of Directive 2008/98/EC." FACT: "This should not apply to certain by-products of the manufacturing process." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Annex I preamble, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* Recovered paper pulp products and recycled rubber (where entirely from end-of-life material) may fall within this exemption. For the workspace handling recycled commodity shipments, this is a threshold question that determines whether any due diligence obligation attaches.

### Exemption 3: Packing Material Used Exclusively for Transport

FACT (as noted above under Product-Specific Compliance Status): HS 4415 applies "not including packing material used exclusively as packing material to support, protect or carry another product placed on the market." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Annex I, European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Exemption 4: SME Operator Simplified Due Diligence

FACT: "By way of derogation from paragraph 1 of this Article, operators that are SMEs … shall not be required to exercise due diligence for relevant products contained in or made from relevant products that have already been subject to due diligence in accordance with paragraph 1 of this Article and for which a due diligence statement has already been submitted." SME operators "shall provide the competent authorities with the reference number of the due diligence statement upon request." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 4(8), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: The December 2025 amendment additionally provides that "micro and small primary operators will now only have to submit a one-off simplified declaration." *Source: European Parliament — Deforestation Law: Parliament Adopts Changes to Postpone and Simplify Measures, European Parliament, 17 December 2025. https://www.europarl.europa.eu/news/en/press-room/20251211IPR32168/deforestation-law-parliament-adopts-changes-to-postpone-and-simplify-measures

### Exemption 5: Low-Risk Country Simplified Due Diligence

FACT: When all relevant commodities have been produced in countries classified as low risk in accordance with Article 29, operators "shall not be required to fulfil the obligations under Articles 10 and 11," subject to having assessed the complexity of the supply chain and risk of circumvention or mixing. The operator must still make available documentation demonstrating negligible risk of circumvention "upon request." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 13(1), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Operational implication:* This exemption depends entirely on the country benchmarking classification. As of the date of this brief, Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1093 is published but its full country list was not available in the source blocks for this brief. The workspace should retrieve and map this implementing act against its sourcing corridors.

### Exemption 6: FLEGT-Licensed Timber

FACT: "Wood products which fall within the scope of Regulation (EC) No 2173/2005 that are covered by a valid FLEGT license from an operational licensing scheme shall be deemed to comply with Article 3, point (b), of this Regulation." This satisfies only the legality condition (Article 3(b)), not the deforestation-free condition (Article 3(a)). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 10(3), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Exemption 7: Third-Country Operators — Authorised Representative Arrangement

FACT: "An operator that is a natural person or a microenterprise may mandate the next operator or trader further down the supply chain that is not a natural person or a microenterprise to act as an authorised representative." In such cases, "the operator that is a natural person or a microenterprise shall retain responsibility for compliance of the relevant product with Article 3." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 6(3), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Whether the workspace can or should accept a written mandate to act as authorised representative for a non-EU operator or microenterprise — and the liability consequences of doing so — requires counsel review.

## Adjacent Industry Research and Alternatives

### Geolocation and Traceability Technology

FACT: The World Resources Institute reports that "Many [companies] are developing satellite-based systems to verify deforestation-free sourcing, training smallholder farmers to meet EUDR requirements and partnering with governments and civil society organizations to improve data sharing and risk assessment." FACT: "Open-source platforms like WRI's Global Forest Watch are also supporting companies in verifying supply chains." *Source: World Resources Institute — What Is the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)?, WRI, last updated 15 May 2026. https://www.wri.org/insights/explain-eu-deforestation-regulation

FACT: The regulation itself contemplates that geolocation coordinates "that rely on timing, positioning and/or Earth observation could make use of space data and services delivered under the Union's Space programme (EGNOS/Galileo and Copernicus)." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Recital (49), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

### Certification and Third-Party Schemes

FACT: The regulation allows that "certification or other third-party verified schemes could be used in the risk assessment procedure. They should not, however, substitute the operator's responsibility as regards due diligence." FACT: "Complementary information on compliance … may include information supplied by certification or other third-party verified schemes, including voluntary schemes recognised by the Commission under Article 30(5) of Directive(EU) 2018/2001." *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Recital (52) and Article 10(2)(n), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces whose clients rely on existing commodity certification (Rainforest Alliance for cocoa and coffee, RSPO for palm oil, FSC/PEFC for wood), these schemes may support the risk assessment step but do not replace the full due diligence obligation, including geolocation data collection and the due diligence statement submission. The workspace should not represent certification as a substitute for EUDR compliance to clients.

### Producer Country Preparedness

FACT: The WRI reports that producer countries including Indonesia, Ghana, and Vietnam are "investing in government-led efforts to make information available to companies that must comply with the regulation." FACT: "Vietnam has shifted its focus from illegal logging to broader deforestation risks in agriculture since the regulation's introduction. In 2023, the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development adopted a national action plan prioritizing sustainable agricultural transformation. The following year, it launched a traceability system for coffee farms, piloting geospatial verification in key producing provinces." *Source: World Resources Institute — What Is the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR)?, WRI, last updated 15 May 2026. https://www.wri.org/insights/explain-eu-deforestation-regulation

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with trade lanes through Vietnam (coffee, rubber) and Indonesia (palm oil, rubber), supplier data availability is improving. The workspace can credibly tell clients that geolocation data from Vietnamese coffee or Indonesian palm oil suppliers is becoming more systematically available, reducing the friction cost of due diligence data collection.

**Penalty note (required slot):** FACT: Penalties include "fines proportionate to the environmental damage and the value of the relevant commodities or relevant products concerned … in the case of a legal person, the maximum amount of such a fine shall be at least **4% of the operator's or trader's total annual Union-wide turnover** in the financial year preceding the fining decision … and shall be increased, where necessary, to exceed the potential economic benefit gained." Additional penalties include: confiscation of products and revenues; temporary exclusion from public procurement for a maximum of 12 months; temporary prohibition from placing or exporting relevant products (serious or repeated infringements); and prohibition from using simplified due diligence (serious or repeated infringements). *Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Article 25(2), European Parliament and Council, 31 May 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/HTML/?uri=CELEX%3A32023R1115

FACT: The EUR-Lex summary confirms: "These penalties should include, among other things, fines proportionate to the environmental damage and the value of the commodities or products, which must be set at a level of at least 4% of the operators' annual turnover in the EU." *Source: EUR-Lex Summary of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115, Publications Office of the European Union, last updated 17 February 2026. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/fighting-deforestation-and-forest-degradation.html
