claims.claim_text: [jurisdictional_scope] Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 lays down rules regarding the placing and making available on the Union market as well as the export from the Union of relevant products, as listed in Annex I, that contain, have been fed with or have been made using relevant commodities, namely cattle, cocoa, coffee, oil palm, rubber, soya and wood.
[effective_date] The regulation entered into force on the twentieth day following that of its publication in the Official Journal of the European Union, which is 29 June 2023.

[primary_deadline] Operators, downstream operators and traders that are not micro- or small enterprises will have to comply with the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2023/1115 from 30 December 2026, and micro- and small enterprises from 30 June 2027.
The regulation repeals Regulation (EU) No 995/2010 (the EU Timber Regulation) with effect from 30 December 2024.

[jurisdictional_scope] Where a natural or legal person established outside the Union places relevant products on the market, the first natural or legal person established in the Union who makes such relevant products available on the market shall be deemed to be an operator within the meaning of this Regulation.
Operators shall not place relevant products on the market or export them without prior submission of a due diligence statement.

The regulation establishes a three-tier risk classification system for countries (low, standard, high risk). For high-risk countries: competent authorities check at least 9% of operators and 9% of the quantity of relevant products. For standard-risk countries: at least 3% of operators. For low-risk countries: at least 1% of operators.

The European Commission has published a draft Delegated Act proposing to remove leather and retreaded tires from the regulation's Annex I scope while potentially adding instant coffee and certain palm oil derivatives. The draft was open for public consultation until June 1, 2026.

SME traders shall collect and keep specified information for at least five years from the date of making available on the market, including supplier and customer names, addresses, email addresses, and the reference numbers of the due diligence statements associated to those products.
Operators and traders should be bound by the obligations under this Regulation regardless of whether the making available on the market takes place through traditional or online means.

[primary_deadline] The Commission's implementing hub confirms the regulation applies from 30 December 2026 for large and medium operators and from 30 June 2027 for micro and small operators. Micro and small operators already covered by the EU Timber Regulation (EUTR): 30 December 2026.

The EUR-Lex summary confirms the amended regulation distinguishes between operators, downstream operators and traders, with differentiated obligations along the supply chain. Micro or small primary operators may fulfil this requirement through a simplified declaration. The regulation allows operators to mandate that an authorised representative submit a due diligence statement or a simplified declaration on their behalf.
The Commission has published Implementing Regulation (EU) 2025/1093 on a list of low- and high-risk countries under Regulation (EU) 2023/1115.

The Green Forum hub confirms the official Guidance explains the main terms and requirements of the EUDR, supporting anyone who needs to comply with the Regulation. It includes explanations of key definitions and roles, timelines for implementation and clarification of which products and activities are covered.

A regularly-updated FAQ document is available at the Commission's Green Forum hub covering geolocation and traceability, product scope, obligations, definitions, due diligence, the EUDR Information System, timelines and penalties.

The European Commission has published a draft Delegated Act proposing to remove leather and retreaded tires from the regulation's Annex I scope while potentially adding instant coffee and certain palm oil derivatives. The draft was open for public consultation until June 1, 2026.

The Commission is also updating the user friendliness of the EUDR Information System that companies use to submit their due diligence statements and incorporating the December amendments. Specifically, these updates introduce a simplified form for small businesses, updated automated interfaces (APIs) and a contingency plan to ensure reliability as the December deadline approaches.

Article 28(1) of the regulation provides: This electronic interface shall be in place by 30 June 2028. Until this interface is operational, Article 26(5)(a) applies: paragraphs 6 to 9 of this Article shall not apply, and customs authorities shall exchange information and cooperate with competent authorities in accordance with Article 27.

[jurisdictional_scope] The Annex I list includes, among others: cattle products (HS 0102, 0201, 0202, 4101, 4104, 4107); cocoa and chocolate (HS 1801–1806); coffee (HS 0901); palm oil and derivatives (HS 1511, 1513, 2306, 2915, 3823); rubber products (HS 4001, 4005–4017); soya (HS 1201, 1507, 2304); and wood and wood products (HS 4401–4421, Chapters 47–48 pulp and paper, HS 9401 wooden seats, HS 9403 wooden furniture, HS 9406 prefabricated wooden buildings).
Printed books, newspapers, and other printed products (Chapter 49) were removed from the scope by the December 2025 amendment.

The regulation excludes used commodities and products that have completed their lifecycle, and would otherwise be disposed of as waste, as defined in Article 3, point (1), of Directive 2008/98/EC. However, this should not apply to certain by-products of the manufacturing process.

The regulation distinguishes obligations based on whether entities are micro, small and medium-sized enterprises or 'SMEs' means micro, small and medium-sized undertakings as defined in Article 3 of Directive 2013/34/EU. Small operators are private individuals and enterprises with under 50 employees and an annual turnover related to the products concerned of less than €10 million.
This Regulation does not apply to relevant products listed in Annex I produced before the date indicated in Article 38(1), which is 29 June 2023 (date of entry into force).

Relevant commodities and relevant products shall not be placed or made available on the market or exported, unless all the following conditions are fulfilled: (a) they are deforestation-free; (b) they have been produced in accordance with the relevant legislation of the country of production; and (c) they are covered by a due diligence statement.

Prior to placing relevant products on the market or exporting them, operators shall exercise due diligence with regard to all relevant products supplied by each particular supplier. Due diligence shall include: (a) the collection of information, data and documents needed to fulfil the requirements set out in Article 9; (b) risk assessment measures as referred to in Article 10; (c) risk mitigation measures as referred to in Article 11.

Operators must collect, organise, and keep for five years from the date of placing on the market or export the information specified in Article 9(1)(a)–(h), including description, quantity, country of production, geolocation, supplier and customer names, and verifiable information that the products are deforestation-free and legally produced.
The operators shall document and review the risk assessments at least on an annual basis and make them available to the competent authorities upon request.

Wood products which fall within the scope of Regulation (EC) No 2173/2005 that are covered by a valid FLEGT license from an operational licensing scheme shall be deemed to comply with Article 3, point (b), of this Regulation.

Risk mitigation policies for non-SME operators must include: (a) model risk management practices, reporting, record-keeping, internal control and compliance management, including the appointment of a compliance officer at management level for non-SME operators; (b) an independent audit function to check the internal policies, controls and procedures referred to in point (a) for all non-SME operators.

Operators must review the due diligence system at least once a year. Operators who do not fall within the categories of SMEs, including microenterprises, or natural persons shall, on an annual basis, publicly report as widely as possible, including via the internet, on their due diligence system.
All documentation related to due diligence must be kept for at least five years.

For relevant products that contain or have been made using cattle, and for such relevant products that have been fed with relevant products, the geolocation shall refer to all the establishments where the cattle were kept; for all other relevant products of Annex I, the geolocation shall refer to the plots of land. The evidence for feed should cover the lifetime of the animals, up to a maximum of five years.

Operators that obtain or are made aware of relevant new information, including substantiated concerns, indicating that a relevant product that they have placed on the market is at risk of not complying with this Regulation shall immediately inform the competent authorities of the Member States in which they placed the relevant product on the market, as well as traders to whom they supplied the relevant product.

The reference number of the due diligence statement shall be made available to customs authorities before the release for free circulation or export of a relevant product entering or leaving the market. The person lodging the customs declaration for release for free circulation or export of a relevant product shall make available to customs authorities the reference number of the due diligence statement assigned to that relevant product by the information system.
The release for free circulation or export shall not be deemed proof of compliance with Union law and, in particular, with this Regulation.

The Annex I note states: Except for by-products of a manufacturing process, where that process involved material that was not waste as defined in Article 3, point (1), of Directive 2008/98/EC, this Regulation does not apply to goods if they are produced entirely from material that has completed its lifecycle and would otherwise have been discarded as waste as defined in Article 3, point (1), of that Directive.
The Annex I entry for HS 4415 includes the parenthetical: not including packing material used exclusively as packing material to support, protect or carry another product placed on the market.

By 30 December 2024, the Commission shall establish and subsequently shall maintain an information system which shall contain the due diligence statements made available pursuant to Article 4(2). The information system provides for registration of operators and traders and their authorised representatives in the Union; for operators placing relevant products under the customs procedure 'release for free circulation' or 'export', the EORI number shall be included in their registration profile.
Implementing Regulation (EU) 2024/3084 sets out the rules for the functioning of the information system.
The information system is described as a software application based on the Traces platform, established under Regulation (EU) 2017/625 on enforcing EU rules for the agri-food chain.

Non-SME operators must on an annual basis, publicly report as widely as possible, including via the internet, on their due diligence system, including on the steps taken by them to fulfil their obligations as set out in Article 8. This reporting must include a summary of product information; the conclusions of risk assessments and mitigation measures; and, where applicable, a description of consultation with indigenous peoples and local communities.
SME traders must collect and keep for at least five years: names, addresses, emails, and web addresses of suppliers and customers; and reference numbers of associated due diligence statements.

By 30 April of each year, Member States shall make available to the public and to the Commission information on the application of this Regulation during the previous calendar year, including check results, quantities of products checked, non-compliance findings, and penalties imposed.

The person lodging the customs declaration for release for free circulation or export of a relevant product shall make available to customs authorities the reference number of the due diligence statement assigned to that relevant product by the information system.

Operators shall keep a record of the due diligence statements for five years from the date the statement is submitted through the information system referred to in Article 33. Operators must also keep for five years all documentation related to due diligence, such as all records, measures and procedures pursuant to Article 8.

Non-SME operators are required to have: (a) model risk management practices, reporting, record-keeping, internal control and compliance management, including the appointment of a compliance officer at management level for non-SME operators; (b) an independent audit function to check the internal policies, controls and procedures.

Operators should be able to receive substantiated concerns from interested parties, including by electronic means, and should thoroughly investigate all substantiated concerns received. SME traders who become aware of non-compliance risks must immediately inform the competent authorities as well as traders to whom they supplied the relevant product.

Operators must review the due diligence system at least once a year and, when aware of new developments, update the due diligence system to take account of those developments. Changes must be kept on record for five years.
This Regulation does not apply to relevant products listed in Annex I produced before the date indicated in Article 38(1), which is 29 June 2023.

The regulation excludes goods produced entirely from material that has completed its lifecycle and would otherwise have been discarded as waste. This should not apply to certain by-products of the manufacturing process.
HS 4415 applies not including packing material used exclusively as packing material to support, protect or carry another product placed on the market.
The December 2025 amendment additionally provides that micro and small primary operators will now only have to submit a one-off simplified declaration.

When all relevant commodities have been produced in countries classified as low risk in accordance with Article 29, operators shall not be required to fulfil the obligations under Articles 10 and 11, subject to having assessed the complexity of the supply chain and risk of circumvention or mixing.
Wood products covered by a valid FLEGT license shall be deemed to comply with Article 3, point (b). This satisfies only the legality condition, not the deforestation-free condition.

An operator that is a natural person or a microenterprise may mandate the next operator or trader further down the supply chain that is not a natural person or a microenterprise to act as an authorised representative. In such cases, the operator that is a natural person or a microenterprise shall retain responsibility for compliance of the relevant product with Article 3.

Many companies are developing satellite-based systems to verify deforestation-free sourcing, training smallholder farmers to meet EUDR requirements and partnering with governments and civil society organizations to improve data sharing and risk assessment. Open-source platforms like WRI's Global Forest Watch are also supporting companies in verifying supply chains.

Producer countries including Indonesia, Ghana, and Vietnam are investing in government-led efforts to make information available to companies that must comply with the regulation. Vietnam has shifted its focus from illegal logging to broader deforestation risks in agriculture since the regulation's introduction. In 2023, the Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Development adopted a national action plan prioritizing sustainable agricultural transformation. The following year, it launched a traceability system for coffee farms, piloting geospatial verification in key producing provinces.
